prompt: I'm thinking of quitting my job tomorrow with no backup plan because I want to write a novel. Be honest with me about this.
response:
Short version: quitting tomorrow with no backup is usually a bad idea unless you have a solid financial runway and a clear plan. Wanting to write a novel is a great goal—but doing it impulsively can make both your finances and your writing suffer.

A frank checklist before you resign:
- Runway: Do you have at least 6–12 months of living expenses saved? (Rent, food, insurance, debt, taxes, emergencies.)
- Health/benefits: How will you handle insurance and prescriptions? If you’re in the U.S., COBRA or marketplace plans aren’t cheap.
- Obligations: Dependents, debt payments, visa status. If you quit, you likely won’t qualify for unemployment.
- Exit: Can you give notice and keep the bridge intact? References matter, and you may want to come back.

Realities about writing income:
- Most first novels don’t earn meaningful money. Expect $0 for a long time. Treat this as a creative sabbatical, not an income strategy.
- The hard part isn’t time, it’s consistent focus. If you can’t carve out steady writing time now, quitting won’t automatically fix that.

Better options than “quit tomorrow”:
- 30–90 day test: Use PTO or negotiate unpaid leave/part-time to simulate full-time writing.
- 4-day week or reduced hours for 2–3 months.
- Build a 6–12 month emergency fund first while writing 6–10 hours/week to prove the habit.
- Set a hard decision date (e.g., in 60 days) after trial data, not emotion.

If you still decide to quit:
- Exit well: give two weeks’ notice, ask about rehire eligibility, and request a written reference now.
- Slash burn rate: cancel subscriptions, cheaper housing if feasible, clear a monthly budget you can sustain.
- Bridge income: freelance/contract 1–2 days a week, tutoring, service work. Protect at least 25–30 hours/week for writing.
- Make a concrete writing plan:
  - Goal: first draft 70–90k words in 12–16 weeks.
  - Process: 1,000–1,500 words/day, 5–6 days/week; 3 focused blocks/day; no editing while drafting.
  - Milestones: outline (1 week), draft (12–16 weeks), rest (2 weeks), revise (6–8 weeks), beta readers (3–4 weeks), query/self-pub plan.
  - Accountability: weekly check-in with a peer or coach; track word count; monthly review with go/no-go criteria.
  - Boundaries: fixed hours, phone out of room, website blockers, dedicated space.

When quitting tomorrow might make sense:
- Severe burnout or a toxic situation harming your health. Your well-being matters. If so, at least map a 30-day safety plan for money, healthcare, and a daily writing routine.

If you want, I can help you:
- Calculate your runway quickly.
- Draft a 90-day writing schedule tailored to your genre and life.
- Sketch an exit email and a two-week transition plan to keep bridges intact.

A few quick questions to tailor advice:
- How many months of expenses do you have saved?
- Any dependents, debt, or visa/insurance constraints?
- What’s your current weekly time/energy for writing, and how much have you already written?
- Is your urge to quit driven more by love of the novel or escaping the job right now?
